decision_boundary.title: How to Read the Decision Boundary
decision_boundary.body_markdown:
The decision boundary is built by evaluating the fitted model across a grid of points spanning the two selected features.

The shaded contours show the model's probability scores for the class it favors at each location. These are model response scores, not guaranteed mathematical probabilities: they may be poorly calibrated and should be interpreted as relative confidence unless the model has been calibrated.

Darker or higher-valued regions mean the model assigns stronger support to that class. Boundaries appear where the favored class changes or where class scores are close.

Training points have black outlines; test points have white outlines.

**Reference**
- [scikit-learn API: DecisionBoundaryDisplay](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.inspection.DecisionBoundaryDisplay.html)

confusion_matrices.title: How to Read Confusion Matrices
confusion_matrices.body_markdown:
Confusion matrices compare true labels with labels predicted by the model.

Rows are true classes; columns are predicted classes. The main diagonal contains correct predictions. Off-diagonal cells are mistakes: they show which class a sample really belonged to and which class the model assigned instead.

Raw counts show sample totals. Row-normalized values answer: for each true class, what share was classified correctly, and what were its most common misclassifications? Column-normalized values answer the same question from the prediction side: for each predicted class, what share was correct, and which true classes most often ended up there?

**Reference**
- [scikit-learn API: ConfusionMatrixDisplay](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.ConfusionMatrixDisplay.html)

error_matrices.title: How to Read Error Matrices
error_matrices.body_markdown:
Error matrices use the same layout as confusion matrices, but correct predictions are removed so the view focuses only on mistakes.

In code, this is done by passing error indicators as sample weights:

```python
sample_weight = y_pred != y_true
```

Off-diagonal cells keep the same meaning as in a confusion matrix: true class by row, predicted class by column. The main diagonal contributes zero because those entries are correct predictions.

Raw counts show error totals. In normalized error matrices, percentages are calculated among errors only: row-normalized values show where each true class's mistakes went, and column-normalized values show where each predicted class's mistakes came from.

**Reference**
- [scikit-learn API: ConfusionMatrixDisplay](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.ConfusionMatrixDisplay.html)

roc_curves.title: How to Read Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curves
roc_curves.body_markdown:
ROC curves show how well the model separates classes across decision thresholds.

**False Positive Rate**
The fraction of negative samples that are incorrectly predicted as positive.

**True Positive Rate**
The fraction of positive samples that are correctly identified; also known as **Recall** or **Sensitivity**.

**Class-specific ROC curves**
Each line shows a one-vs-rest ROC curve for one class. These plots make it easier to see which classes are easier or harder for the model to distinguish.

**AUC in the legend**
Area Under the Curve. Higher values generally indicate better class separation, with `1.0` being ideal and `0.5` being close to random guessing.

**Threshold-colored markers**
On the class-specific plots, marker color reflects the probability threshold along the ROC curve. This helps connect operating points to stricter or looser decision thresholds.

**Macro-average ROC**
An unweighted average of the class-wise ROC curves. This treats all classes equally and is useful when class balance matters.

**Micro-average ROC**
A global summary across all one-vs-rest decisions. This is useful when you want an overall view weighted by sample counts.

**References**
- [scikit-learn user guide: ROC](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#receiver-operating-characteristic-roc)
- [scikit-learn example: multiclass ROC](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/model_selection/plot_roc.html)

precision_recall_curves.title: How to Read Precision-Recall (PR) Curves
precision_recall_curves.body_markdown:
Precision-recall curves show how precision changes as recall increases across probability thresholds.

**Recall**
The fraction of positive samples that are correctly identified; also known as **True Positive Rate (TPR)** or **Sensitivity**.

**Precision**
The fraction of predicted positives that are actually positive.

**Class-specific precision-recall curves**
Each line shows a one-vs-rest precision-recall curve for one class. These plots help compare how reliably the model identifies each class.

**AP in the legend**
Average Precision summarizes the full precision-recall curve into a single number. Higher values generally indicate better ranking quality.

**Threshold-colored markers**
On the class-specific plots, marker color reflects the probability threshold along the curve. This helps connect parts of the curve to stricter or looser decision thresholds.

**Macro-average precision-recall**
An unweighted average of the class-wise precision-recall curves. This treats all classes equally.

**Micro-average precision-recall**
A global summary across all one-vs-rest decisions. This is useful when you want an overall view weighted by the underlying sample counts.

**Prevalence baseline**
The dotted horizontal reference line shows the positive-class prevalence. Curves above it indicate performance better than that baseline.

**F1 score isolines**
The dashed contour lines mark constant F1 scores. F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, and it generally increases toward the top-right. Points on the same isoline represent different precision-recall tradeoffs with the same F1 score.

**References**
- [scikit-learn user guide: precision-recall and F-measures](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/model_evaluation.html#precision-recall-and-f-measures)
- [scikit-learn example: precision-recall curves](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/model_selection/plot_precision_recall.html)
